clue: Occupying most of the Southern Cone, this is South America's second-largest country after Brazil.
proposed_clue: Flanked by the Andes to the west and the Atlantic coast to the east, this country occupies most of South America's Southern Cone and is the continent's second-largest after Brazil.
lessons: Lead with a vivid, concrete hook — not provenance or category labels
why: Original opens with a bare geographic category label; Andes/Atlantic framing gives the clue a concrete geographic image without changing its difficulty.

clue: This landlocked South American nation spans an Andean west and tropical lowlands, including the high-altitude Altiplano.
proposed_clue: This landlocked South American country spans from the Andean Altiplano in the west to tropical lowlands in the east.
why: 'Spans an Andean west' is grammatically awkward, and 'high-altitude Altiplano' is redundant — Altiplano already means high plateau.

clue: Taking the throne in 1553, she was the first queen to rule England in her own right.
proposed_clue: Taking the throne in 1553, this monarch was the first woman to rule England in her own right.
why: Uses 'she' as the answer proxy rather than the required 'this [noun]'.

clue: Henry VIII's second wife, she was executed for treason in 1536, with the circumstances of her marriage regarded as a key catalyst for the English Reformation.
proposed_clue: Henry VIII's second wife, this queen consort was executed for treason in 1536; the controversy surrounding their marriage is regarded as a key catalyst for the English Reformation.
why: Uses 'she' as the answer proxy, and the trailing participial 'with the circumstances of her marriage regarded as' is passive and awkward; recast as an independent clause with a semicolon.

clue: The greatest novelist of the Victorian era by many accounts, this English author left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
proposed_clue: This English Victorian novelist left school at 12 when his father was imprisoned for debt, forcing him to work in a boot-blacking factory.
why: 'The greatest novelist of the Victorian era by many accounts' is a vague, hedged superlative that earns nothing; cutting it and folding 'Victorian' into the noun yields a tighter, stronger opening.

clue: An example of the literary nonsense genre, this 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.
proposed_clue: This 1865 Lewis Carroll children's novel follows a young girl who tumbles down a rabbit hole into a fantasy world of anthropomorphic creatures.
lessons: Lead with a vivid, concrete hook — not provenance or category labels
why: 'An example of the literary nonsense genre' is a generic category label that opens with nothing concrete; dropping it lets the story detail lead.

clue: The American novelist who coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection achieved transient success in the 1920s but received critical acclaim only after his death.
proposed_clue: This American novelist coined the term 'Jazz Age' in a 1922 short story collection, achieving transient success in the 1920s but receiving critical acclaim only after his death.
why: The clue uses 'The American novelist who…' with no 'this [noun]' answer-proxy marker, which every other clue in the set has; restructuring around 'this American novelist' fixes the Jeopardy-format defect.

clue: Arthur Miller's 1949 Broadway tragedy follows Willy Loman, a middle-aged traveling businessman slipping into senility who confronts the chasm between his failed life and the version of the American Dream he had always imagined for himself.
proposed_clue: Arthur Miller's 1949 Broadway tragedy follows Willy Loman, a middle-aged man slipping into senility who confronts the chasm between his failed life and the American Dream he had always imagined for himself.
lessons: Cut the giveaway — and hidden or phonetic leaks
why: 'Traveling businessman' is a near-synonym for 'salesman' (the answer's key word); 'the version of the American Dream' also trimmed for slight bloat.

clue: Its name is the Latin word for 'trumpet,' though this large brass instrument plays in the bass-to-contrabass range and descended from earlier instruments called the ophicleide and the serpent.
proposed_clue: Descended from earlier instruments called the ophicleide and the serpent, this large brass instrument plays in the bass-to-contrabass range of the orchestra.
lessons: Cut the giveaway — and hidden or phonetic leaks
why: 'Its name is the Latin word for trumpet' directly reveals the answer to anyone who knows that 'tuba' is the Latin word for trumpet — etymology removed entirely.

clue: Frank Wedekind's 1891 German play about adolescent sexuality, long considered too scandalous for the stage, was adapted into a 2006 Broadway rock musical that won eight Tony Awards including Best Musical.
proposed_clue: Frank Wedekind's 1891 German play about adolescent sexuality, long considered too scandalous for the stage, was adapted into this 2006 Broadway rock musical that won eight Tony Awards including Best Musical.
why: Missing 'this [noun]' answer proxy — the musical was described as 'a 2006 Broadway rock musical' with no demonstrative marking it as the answer.

clue: She used a famous 'violinist' thought experiment in her essay 'A Defense of Abortion.'
proposed_clue: This philosopher used a famous 'violinist' thought experiment in her essay 'A Defense of Abortion.'
why: Opens with pronoun 'She' rather than 'this [noun]'; minimal fix preserves all content.

clue: Often summarized as treating others as you would wish to be treated, this maxim shares its name with a precious metal.
proposed_clue: Often summarized as treating others as you would wish to be treated, this universal maxim appears in nearly every major religion and moral tradition.
why: 'Shares its name with a precious metal' explicitly telegraphs 'golden' in the answer; replaced with a genuine distinguishing fact that does not leak the name.

clue: This ethical stance prioritizes actions that benefit the long-run future and far-future generations.
proposed_clue: This ethical view holds that reducing existential risk and improving outcomes for future generations should take priority over near-term concerns.
why: 'Long-run future' echoes 'long' in the answer and is redundant with 'far-future generations'; rewrite removes both the word-leak and the repetition.

clue: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, she came to symbolize Rome's hegemony and right to rule; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology of her own, she left behind a name now borne by far more women than worshippers.
proposed_clue: Romanized from the Greek Nike during the First Punic War, this goddess came to symbolize Rome's hegemony and right to rule; a deified abstraction with virtually no mythology of her own, she left behind a name now borne by far more women than worshippers.
why: Opens with pronoun 'she' after the participial phrase; minimal fix to 'this goddess' properly marks the answer proxy while preserving the elegant closing line intact.

clue: The wings of these tiny birds can beat up to about 80 times per second.
proposed_clue: The wings of these tiny birds beat up to 80 times per second.
why: 'up to about' is a double hedge — 'up to' already implies approximation; 'can' is also redundant given 'up to'.

clue: Native to much of South America, it is the largest living rodent.
proposed_clue: Native to much of South America, this animal is the largest living rodent.
why: Opens with pronoun 'it' — Jeopardy clues must mark the answer proxy with 'this [noun]', not a pronoun.

clue: This Hall of Famer stole a record 1,406 bases, far more than any other player in MLB history.
proposed_clue: This Hall of Famer stole a record 1,406 bases in his major-league career.
why: 'Far more than any other player in MLB history' is pure redundancy — 'record' already encodes that.

clue: Coined by the writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, this term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a misinterpreted song lyric — arose when she reinterpreted a ballad's line 'laid him on the green' as the name of a slain noblewoman.
proposed_clue: Coined by the writer Sylvia Wright in 1954, this term for a misheard word or phrase — most often a misinterpreted song lyric — arose when she mistook a line from a Scottish ballad for the name of a slain noblewoman.
lessons: Cut the giveaway — and hidden or phonetic leaks
why: The quoted phrase 'laid him on the green' phonetically implies the answer ('on the green' → '…ndegreen'); removing the direct quote preserves the charming origin story without the leak.
